scenario: A new analyst at Cascade Steel monitors Modbus TCP traffic on the historian. She sees repeated function code 03 requests from an engineering workstation to a PLC.
question: What does Modbus function code 03 do?
options: It writes a single coil value to a specific PLC output register.
It reads the values of multiple holding registers from a PLC or RTU.
It forces a PLC into diagnostic mode to report internal memory errors.
It resets the PLC communication stack and re-establishes the Modbus session.
aria_hint: This is a read operation — focus on what it retrieves, not what it changes.
debrief: FC03 reads holding registers — the most common Modbus data source, used for process values, setpoints, and status. FC01 reads coils (discrete outputs). FC05 writes a single coil. FC06 writes a single holding register. There is no built-in diagnostic or session-reset function code in standard Modbus; Options C and D describe capabilities the protocol does not have.

scenario: During a threat hunt at Redrock Oil & Gas, analysts find an attacker replaying captured DNP3 traffic to a substation RTU. The RTU accepts and executes the replayed commands without error.
question: Why does the RTU accept replayed DNP3 commands without error?
options: The RTU firmware has a bug that disables sequence number checking.
DNP3 uses TCP, which does not provide application-layer sequence protection.
The attacker used a zero-day in the DNP3 stack to bypass session validation.
Standard DNP3 has no native message authentication — any correctly formatted packet is accepted.
aria_hint: Think about what DNP3 was designed for — reliable data transfer — not about who is authorised to send commands.
debrief: DNP3 was designed in the early 1990s for reliable serial communication between trusted devices. It has no native message authentication in its base spec — any correctly formatted packet sent to the RTU will be executed. DNP3 Secure Authentication v5 (IEEE 1815-2012) was added later as an optional extension but is not widely deployed. Option A implies a firmware bug, but this is by design. Option B is wrong — TCP sequence numbers protect transport-layer ordering, not application-layer command authentication. Option C implies a zero-day; the scenario shows standard protocol behaviour.

sources.text: CISA — Improving ICS Cybersecurity with Defence-in-Depth (PDF)

scenario: An attacker on a compromised engineering workstation at Apex Energy sends PROFINET DCP Identify All requests across the control network. Within seconds, they have a full list of all PROFINET devices — names, IPs, MACs, and vendor details — without sending a single exploit.
question: Which MITRE ATT&CK for ICS technique does this PROFINET DCP scan represent?
options: T0846.003 — Remote System Discovery: Multicast Discovery, using PROFINET DCP to enumerate OT assets.
T0888 — Remote System Information Discovery, extracting detailed firmware and OS info from hosts.
T0840 — Network Connection Enumeration, mapping communication patterns between PROFINET devices.
T0817 — Drive-by Compromise, exploiting a PROFINET stack vulnerability on the workstation.
aria_hint: DCP Identify All sends to an Ethernet multicast address — every PROFINET device responds. This is passive enumeration, not exploitation.
debrief: PROFINET DCP Identify All sends to an Ethernet multicast address (01:0E:CF:00:00:00); every PROFINET device on the segment responds with its name, IP, MAC, and vendor. No exploit is needed — the attacker used a built-in mechanism. This is T0846.003 Remote System Discovery: Multicast Discovery, the most specific sub-technique for multicast-based enumeration protocols like PROFINET DCP. T0888 (Option B) goes further — extracting detailed firmware and OS info from individual hosts; DCP only returns network presence data. T0840 (Option C) enumerates active connections using tools like Netstat, not device presence via multicast. Option D is an initial access technique, not a discovery technique.

sources.text: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0846 Remote System Discovery
MITRE ATT&CK ICS — T0846.003 Multicast Discovery (PROFINET DCP)

scenario: Analysts at Northfield Power discover INCONTROLLER-style tooling sending unsolicited Codesys runtime commands to Schneider Electric Modicon PLCs on the OT network. The PLCs accept the commands and alter programme execution without any operator approval.
question: What is the attacker most likely achieving by sending these unauthorised Codesys commands?
options: Crashing the PLCs by flooding them with malformed Codesys connection requests.
Extracting PLC firmware images for offline vulnerability analysis.
Establishing direct programme control to modify PLC logic and process behaviour.
Pivoting through the PLCs to reach devices on isolated network segments.
aria_hint: Codesys runtime commands execute directly on PLC logic — focus on what accepting unauthorised commands lets the attacker do.
debrief: INCONTROLLER's EVILSCHOLAR module used Codesys runtime protocols to send unauthorised commands directly to Schneider Electric Modicon PLCs — bypassing all operator controls. This is T0855 Unauthorized Command Message: the attacker gains direct control over programme logic, setpoints, and process behaviour without needing to exploit a vulnerability. Option A (DoS flooding) uses blunt methods; INCONTROLLER was built for precise, targeted manipulation. Option B (firmware extraction) uses file-read or upload services, not runtime commands. Option D (pivoting) is a lateral movement goal — INCONTROLLER's purpose here was direct process control, not network traversal.

sources.text: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0855 Unauthorized Command Message
CISA Advisory AA22-103A — APT Cyber Tools Targeting ICS/SCADA (INCONTROLLER/PIPEDREAM)

scenario: During a security assessment at Stonebridge Manufacturing, the assessor sends a Modbus FC06 Write Single Register command from a laptop directly to a production PLC. The PLC accepts and executes the write with no authentication challenge.
question: Why does the PLC accept the write command with no authentication?
options: The PLC is in maintenance mode, which disables authentication for service access.
Modbus has no native authentication — any reachable host can send valid commands.
The assessor's laptop is on the engineering VLAN, which is trusted by the PLC.
The PLC's authentication module is disabled when no active session exists.
aria_hint: Modbus was designed in 1979 for trusted serial links — authentication was never part of the specification.
debrief: Modbus was developed for serial RS-232 communication between trusted devices in isolated environments. It has no authentication, no encryption, and no source verification — any host that reaches port 502 (Modbus TCP) can read or write registers. This is not a misconfiguration; it is how the protocol works. Option A implies maintenance mode disables authentication, but Modbus has no authentication to disable in any mode. Option C (VLAN trust) is a network segmentation concept, not a Modbus protocol feature. Option D invents an authentication module that standard Modbus does not have.

scenario: A threat actor on a compromised Level 2 HMI at Vantage Water Authority uses standard Modbus polling tools to map all PLCs on the Level 1 segment, read live process values, and identify setpoint registers. No new tools are installed; no commands are written.
question: Which MITRE ATT&CK for ICS tactic does reading PLC process data represent in this kill chain?
options: TA0104 — Execution, as the attacker is running commands on the PLC.
TA0105 — Inhibit Response Function, as reading setpoints enables future alarm suppression.
TA0109 — Lateral Movement, moving from the compromised HMI to reach the PLCs.
TA0102 — Discovery, gathering intelligence about the process environment before acting.
aria_hint: Reading data to understand the environment — before taking any action — maps to which phase of the kill chain?
debrief: Reading register values and mapping PLCs is intelligence gathering — building a picture of the process before taking disruptive action. This is TA0102 Discovery. Option A (Execution) would apply if the attacker sent commands that change process state; reading registers does not execute anything on the PLC's logic. Option B (Inhibit Response Function) requires an action that suppresses alarms; passive reading does not. Option C (Lateral Movement) describes pivoting from one system to another — the attacker already has network reach to Level 1 from the HMI; what they are doing there is reconnaissance.

sources.text: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — TA0102 Discovery
MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0801 Monitor Process State

scenario: An attacker on a compromised workstation at Cascade Steel sends Modbus FC16 to overwrite the high-temperature alarm setpoint on a furnace PLC — raising it from 850°C to 1200°C. No operator alarms fire. The physical process begins running dangerously hot.
question: Why does this attack succeed silently, and which MITRE technique does it represent?
options: Modbus has no write protection; FC16 writes setpoints silently — this is T0836 Modify Parameter.
FC16 is a diagnostic command that bypasses PLC alarm logic when used in maintenance mode.
The attack succeeded because FC08 first reset the PLC's alarm history register.
FC16 only executes after a matching confirmation message from the HMI supervisor.
aria_hint: Two things happening: why no alarm fired (threshold raised, not crossed), and what technique changed the setpoint.
debrief: FC16 (Write Multiple Registers) can write to any holding register including setpoints — no authentication, no write protection. By raising the alarm threshold, the attacker ensures the process runs hot without crossing the (now falsified) alarm boundary. This is T0836 Modify Parameter — changing configuration values to enable a later physical impact. Option B is wrong: FC16 is a standard write operation, not a diagnostic command, and Modbus has no maintenance mode. Option C invents an alarm reset mechanism that does not exist in standard Modbus. Option D is wrong: Modbus has no confirmation handshake for write commands.

sources.text: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0836 Modify Parameter
